0cb38012-3cf7-46ff-8a1a-3b621fde7996.classification_raw:
Bench Press

1. Feet & base
2. Glutes & legs
3. Arch & lower back position
4. Shoulder blades & upper back
5. Grip & hand placement
6. Bar path
7. Elbow alignment
8. Head & Neck
9. Tempo and control
0cb38012-3cf7-46ff-8a1a-3b621fde7996.response:
Awesome effort—strong press! Here’s a quick tune‑up based on what I see.

What looks good
- Solid upper‑back arch and chest up—you’re creating a platform to press from.
- You’re bringing the bar to the chest instead of hovering.

Main fixes
- Foot position/leg drive: Your feet are splayed far out to the sides and shins aren’t near vertical. Bring each foot back and closer to the bench, heels planted. This lets you drive force up your legs into the arch without your hips wanting to pop up.
- Hips/glutes: On a few reps your hips look like they want to lift—keep your butt down on the pad the whole time. You can still keep the arch while keeping glutes in contact.
- Elbow angle: Your elbows are flaring wide. During the descent, drop them to roughly a 45° angle to your torso. That keeps the shoulders happier and transfers force better.
- Tightness before each rep: After unracking, make sure all four contact points are planted—head, upper back, glutes, and feet—then take a deep breath into your gut and squeeze the bar hard (“bend the bar”) before you lower.
- Bar path consistency: Pick a spot on the ceiling, keep your eyes fixed there, touch the chest without pausing excessively, and drive the bar back toward that same point.

Mental cues
- Heels down, shins nearly vertical, legs close to the bench.
- Pinch shoulder blades together and down; puff the chest.
- Big belly breath—brace hard.
- Bend the bar; grip like you mean it.
- Elbows at ~45° on the way down.
- Eyes locked on one ceiling spot; press back to it.

If these changes make the bar speed slow or form wobble, back the weight off slightly and build back up. Also, near max efforts, have a spotter help with the liftoff (lift out, not up).

04c08a31-4c0c-4d7f-9c75-b5e2c353c3ba.classification_raw:
**Bench Press**

1. Feet & Lower Body
2. Glutes & Back
3. Core Engagement
4. Grip & Wrist Position
5. Elbow Path
6. Bar Path
7. Head & Neck Position
8. Tempo and Control
04c08a31-4c0c-4d7f-9c75-b5e2c353c3ba.response:
Love the effort! You’re clearly getting reps in on the incline press. Here’s what I see.

What looks good
- Head and upper back are staying on the bench through the set.
- You’re controlling the bar through a consistent range.

Main fixes
- Feet/leg drive: Your feet are flared out wide and far in front, which makes you loose. Bring each foot back as far as you comfortably can and keep the legs closer to the bench when viewed from the front. Plant them (heels down if using regulation), push your knees out, squeeze your glutes, and avoid “floppy feet.”
- Upper‑back platform: Your shoulders look relaxed. Pinch your shoulder blades together and plant your upper back firmly against the bench so you have a solid platform to press from.
- Elbow angle: Your elbows are flaring too much. During the descent, drop the elbows to about a 45-degree angle relative to your torso to reduce shoulder stress and improve power.
- Wrist/hand position: Your wrists look bent back. Let the bar sit on the heel of your palm directly over the forearm bones and grip the bar hard so the wrists stay stacked.
- Bar path and eyes: At lockout, move the bar so it’s directly over the line of your shoulder joints. Fix your eyes on a spot on the ceiling there, lower to your chest, touch, and drive the bar back to that same spot. Don’t watch the bar as it moves.

Mental cues
- Feet back and planted; no floppy feet.
- Pinch shoulder blades and stay tight on the bench.
- Bend the bar/rip it in half to tuck elbows ~45° and engage your lats.
- Bar over shoulders at lockout; eyes fixed on your ceiling target.

3c32fb20-05b6-47eb-9ce2-8b6e6fd9da8d.classification_raw:
**Bench Press**

1. Feet placement
2. Hip position
3. Lower back arch
4. Shoulder position
5. Elbow angle
6. Wrist alignment
7. Bar path
8. Tempo and control
3c32fb20-05b6-47eb-9ce2-8b6e6fd9da8d.response:
Love the effort! Here’s a quick form check from what I can see.

What looks good
- Butt and upper back are staying on the bench throughout the rep.
- You’re touching around the mid–lower chest and not bouncing the bar.

Main fixes
- Plant your feet. Your heels keep popping up and the feet shift (“floppy feet”). Slide your feet back only as far as you can while keeping them flat on the floor. This will tighten your whole body and improve leg drive.
- Bring your legs closer to the bench when viewed from the front. Keep heels down (toes can turn out) so you’ve got a solid base.
- Set your upper back tighter. Pinch the shoulder blades together and keep the chest “puffed” so the bar has a stable platform—your shoulders look like they drift forward a bit at lockout.
- Elbow path: keep about a 45° angle on the way down. Think about “bending the bar into a U” or “protect your armpits” to keep the lats engaged and avoid flaring.
- Bar path and eyes: pick a spot on the ceiling, lower to your touch point, and drive the bar back toward that same ceiling spot each rep. This will keep your path consistent.
- Setup details: make sure your eyes start under the barbell, grip hard (you may be stronger with a slightly wider grip), unrack by lifting out—not up—and re-rack only with locked elbows.

Mental cues
- Feet flat. No floppy feet.
- Pinch shoulder blades. Chest up.
- Big breath into your gut, brace.
- Bend the bar / protect your armpits.
- Eyes fixed on one ceiling spot; touch, then drive back to that spot.

Run a couple light sets focusing only on these cues, then build the weight back up. Want me to check a quick video after you plant those heels?

ff1f1755-ffbc-4fae-8e34-a3838f436606.classification_raw:
**Bench Press**

1. Feet & Base
2. Glutes & Legs
3. Lower Back Arch
4. Core & Ribcage
5. Shoulder Position
6. Elbow Position
7. Grip & Wrist
8. Bar Path
9. Head & Neck
10. Tempo and Control
ff1f1755-ffbc-4fae-8e34-a3838f436606.response:
Love that you’re filming your bench—great way to dial things in.

What looks good
- You’re touching the bar to your chest each rep instead of hovering.
- Nice chest-up position with some arch—good start on building a solid base.

Main fixes
- Feet: Your feet are up, so you’re missing the lower‑body base. Plant them on the floor (flat or up on your toes) and keep them planted so you have four points of contact: head, upper back, glutes, and feet. This will stop the “floppy feet” issue and let you press tighter and heavier.
- Grip width: Your grip looks narrower than shoulder width. Set up with roughly shoulder‑width grip to give your pecs better leverage and make the 45° elbow path easier.
- Touch point: You’re touching too high (near the upper chest). Aim to touch the middle of your sternum, a few inches below the clavicles. If you don’t have a spotter to tap that spot, unlock your elbows and let them drop slightly toward your feet on the way down to guide the bar there.
- Elbow angle: Don’t flare straight out. During the descent, drop your elbows to about 45° relative to your torso.
- Bar path and eyes: Pick a spot on the ceiling directly over your shoulders and keep your eyes glued there. Lower to your sternum and then drive the bar back to that ceiling point each rep—don’t watch the bar.
- Unrack: Lift the bar out, not up. If you’re alone, you can keep your butt elevated for the liftoff and set your hips down once the bar is over your shoulders. Use a spotter for heavy efforts.

Mental cues
- Feet planted, glutes down—four points of contact.
- Pinch shoulder blades together and keep them pinched.
- Squeeze the bar hard and “rip it in half.”
- Elbows ~45°; touch mid‑sternum.
- Eyes locked on one ceiling spot; drive the bar back to it.

If you want, send another clip after you plant the feet and widen to shoulder width—I’ll re-check your bar path and elbow angle.

ea282b8e-bfd0-486c-9a85-32bf69f89bbb.user_query: can you help me get better?
ea282b8e-bfd0-486c-9a85-32bf69f89bbb.classification_raw:
Bench Press

1. Feet & base
2. Lower back arch
3. Shoulder positioning
4. Wrist alignment
5. Bar path
6. Elbow angle
7. Head & neck position
8. Bar position on chest
ea282b8e-bfd0-486c-9a85-32bf69f89bbb.response:
Absolutely—let’s dial in your bench!

What looks good
- You’re touching the bar to your chest each rep and keeping a visible arch.
- Grip looks firm and you’re controlling the descent.

Main fixes
- Plant your feet. In the video your feet are splayed wide and the heels/toes look loose between reps. Floppy feet = loose legs and soft core. Keep all four contact points locked in: head, upper back, glutes, and feet.
- Tuck your elbows on the way down. You’re lowering with elbows flared out near 90°. Drop them to about 45° relative to your torso to engage the lats and press more safely and efficiently.
- Use the “up-and-back” bar path. You’re pressing mostly straight up. Fix your eyes on one spot on the ceiling and drive the bar back toward that spot so it returns to the same place every rep.
- Get tighter before each rep. Big breath into your gut, brace, and grip the bar like you’re trying to rip it in half—this keeps the upper back tight and the shoulder blades pinched together to create a solid platform.
- If the bench is slippery and your butt slides, wrap a couple of bungees around the pad so you can stay set when loads get heavier.
- If your core or legs loosen up mid-set, stop and reset—don’t keep pressing with a loose base. Start lighter, groove the pattern, and build back up.

Mental cues
- Feet planted—no floppy feet.
- Pinch shoulder blades and drive your back up into the bar.
- Big breath, brace, crush the bar, “bend it into a U.”
- Protect your armpits—elbows ~45° on the way down.
- Touch, then press up and back to the ceiling spot your eyes are fixed on.

Want me to mark up a still frame with elbow angle and foot position targets?
